entries.title: Jordan faces growing risk of becoming latest frontline state in US-Iran conflict
entries.summary: As Iranian attacks intensify, Jordan is emerging as the latest frontline state in the widening Middle East conflict. Why is the Hashemite Kingdom a target for Tehran?
entries.content:
July 21, 2026

As Iranian attacks intensify, Jordan is emerging as the latest frontline state in the widening Middle East conflict. Why is the Hashemite Kingdom a target for Tehran?

Air raid sirens again sounded in parts of Jordan early Tuesday as Iranian missiles targeted the kingdom.

"The air defense system intercepted and downed tonight three missiles coming from Iranian territory that entered Jordanian airspace and targeted the kingdom's territory," the army said in a statement.

No injuries were reported, but the latest strikes follow a series of attacks that have raised the stakes for one of the United States' closest Arab allies in the past days: Two US service members were killed and around 30 others injured by an Iranian missile strike on a US base in Jordan last week.

Jordan is one of the eight Arab countries that host permanent US military facilities. Some of the other host countries — particularly the Gulf states — have been the targets of Iranian attacks since the United States and Israel launched joint attacks on Iran in late February.

Though Iran launched missiles toward Jordan shortly thereafter, there has since been a lull in direct attacks on the kingdom, which has helped to intercept Iranian missiles launched toward Israel, arguing that it is acting in defense of its own airspace. 

"Iran has reserved itself the right to return fire on any state in the region that hosts US military assets or bases, and Jordan is one of them," Erwin van Veen, a senior research fellow at the Clingendael Institute in The Hague, told DW. "Only the options of Jordan's King Abdullah II are quite limited as Jordan is in a very precarious balance with respect to military and economic dependence on the Americans."

On Sunday, in a first diplomatic escalation, Jordan summoned Iran's top diplomat in Amman to demand an immediate end to what the foreign ministry called "unjustified and blatant Iranian attacks."

Jordan's political and economic tightrope walk

The US is Jordan's largest single foreign supporter. Under a seven-year Memorandum of Understanding signed in 2022, Washington agreed to provide $1.45 billion (€1.27bn) annually in economic and military assistance. In 2025, Congress increased this to $1.65 billion, which was triple the amount from 2010.

Furthermore, a Defense Cooperation Agreement signed in January 2021 grants US forces formalized access to Jordanian military facilities. According to a recent report, there are currently 4,000 US troops, more than in previous years, stationed in Jordan.

In March, however, Jordanian Foreign Minister Ayman Safadi insisted that there were no foreign military bases in the country. Kelly Petillo, program manager for Middle East and North Africa at the European Council on Foreign Relations, said one reason for downplaying foreign military presence in Jordan could be anti-US sentiment.

"Despite Jordan's efforts to remain neutral in this war, existing alliances such as the one with the US not only affect Jordan's fragile security but also cause public discontent," she told DW.

She speculated that Jordan, like its Gulf allies in the region, might be beginning to question whether its alliance with the US was beneficial to the country's stability or actually a cause of instability.

A 2026 poll by Arab Barometer, a nonpartisan public opinion research network, concluded that only 12% of Jordanians view US President Donald Trump's foreign policy favorably.

Jordan-Israel ties

"The other risk for Amman is that Iran's attacks could also place it in the same camp as Israel," Petillo warned. "This impression could cause further discontent among the wider public, creating space for protests as they were already seen during the war in Gaza, when people called for an end to the Israel-Jordan 1994 peace treaty."

In the past three years, relations between Israel and Jordan have become increasingly strained. In November 2023, the two countries recalled their ambassadors after Jordan's condemnation of Israel's conduct during the early days of its war in Gaza, which was unleashed after the Hamas attack on Israel on October 7, 2023. Hamas is considered a terrorist organization by the US, the EU and others.

Jordan has been at the forefront of efforts to push for a two-state solution, which would grant Palestinians their own state alongside Israel.

The Palestinian issue is of particular importance for Jordanian citizens as more than half of the population, including Queen Rania, has Palestinian roots. Around 2.4 million Palestinian refugees live in Jordan, according to the United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East (UNRWA).

But Jordan has repeatedly rejected any more displacement of Palestinians into the kingdom, particularly considering the high cost of hosting refugees whose return is anything but guaranteed.

"Jordan has been faced with a massive decline in USAID funding and the US pullback from UNRWA," Petillo said.

This comes as Jordan continues to struggle with high public debt and unemployment.

Popular anger related to Israel has recently been exacerbated by the expansion of settlements in the occupied West Bank, which the United Nations and rights groups say has accelerated since 2023. Increased settler violence has made life even worse for Palestinians in the West Bank.

According to the aid organization Save the Children, more than 1,000 Palestinian children were displaced by a surge in settler violence in the West Bank in the first six months of 2026. This was half the total number of children forced from their homes by settler violence over the entire previous three-year period.

"As the current Israeli coalition wrote in its own coalition agreement at the start of its tenure in 2022, it ultimately intends to annex the West Bank," Erwin van Veen said.

"Jordan is, in many ways, between a rock and a hard place, as it has little room for manoeuvre — neither toward Israel, Iran nor Washington," he added.

The West Bank, a landlocked territory of about 5,655 square kilometers (2,183 square miles), lies between Israel and Jordan. Israel took control of it from Jordan in the 1967 Six-Day War. According to the United Nations Security Council, "Israel's establishment of settlements in Palestinian territory occupied since 1967, including East Jerusalem, had no legal validity."

Edited by: Anne Thomas, Carla Bleiker

entries.title: Germany records rise in sexual violence against children
entries.summary: Germany's Criminal Police Office has reported over 17,000 cases of sexual violence against children in the past year. Organizations such as Zartbitter provide support to victims.
entries.content:
Germany

Germany records rise in sexual violence against children

July 21, 2026

Germany's Criminal Police Office has reported over 17,000 cases of sexual violence against children in the past year. Organizations such as Zartbitter provide support to victims.

Leonie Kirschstein knew that the number of sexual violence cases involving children and adolescents would not decline. The psychologist works for the registered nonprofit Zartbitter e.V., which has served as a support center for young victims in city of Cologne in western Germany for nearly four decades.  This pioneering organization counsels victims, conducts educational outreach in schools and even stages plays on the topic of sexual violence.

Kirschstein, who has worked for Zartbitter for three years, told DW that the number of unreported cases was much higher: "Crime statistics only include cases that have been reported to the police. But not the ones we deal with every day, such as cases involving peer violence. In many cases, these aren't reported at all, especially when the children are still very young. This group makes up the largest portion of the cases we handle in our counseling process."

Victims of sexual violence in every classroom

The issue of sexual violence is more topical than ever: The World Health Organization estimates that up to 1 million children and adolescents in Germany have experienced or are currently undergoing sexual violence at the hands of adults.

This means that, on average, in every German school classroom, there are one to two children who are victims of sexual violence. Kirschstein, who specializes in psychotherapy with children and adolescents, says: "We receive new cases every day; parents, in particular, call for an initial consultation. Unfortunately, I still very often find the victims wonder whether they themselves did something wrong — that is, whether they said 'no' explicitly and whether their refusal was understood as such by the perpetrator."

German Interior Minister Alexander Dobrindt wants to retain IP addresses

Leonie Kirschstein's observations align with the "Federal Assessment of Sexual Offenses Against Children and Adolescents 2025," which was released on July 21 in Berlin.

It states that the number of cases of sexual abuse of children and adolescents remains alarmingly high and has even risen in many areas. The number of sexual abuse offenses against minors under the age of 14 alone rose to 17,126 cases from 16,354 in 2024.

New record highs have also been reached for crimes involving "child pornography" and for the sexual abuse of children without physical contact.

German Interior Minister Alexander Dobrindt is sounding the alarm: "The high number of cases of sexual abuse of children and adolescents shows that we must protect our children even better — especially in the digital world," he said. "Perpetrators must not be allowed to feel safe and hide in the anonymity of the internet. In the fight against these repugnant crimes, our law enforcement agencies need effective tools such as IP address logging — to identify perpetrators, prosecute them consistently, and put an end to active abuse," Dobrindt told the media.

BKA President warns of 'cybergrooming'

To better prosecute cybercrime, the federal government is currently pushing for a law allowing the retention of IP addresses for three months. Dobrindt is confident that such a law could be passed as early as this fall. He has also called for the establishment of the post of European Commissioner for the Prevention of Sexual Abuse of Children and Adolescents.

Holger Münch, the president of the Federal Criminal Police Office (BKA), meanwhile, has warned against "cybergrooming." This refers to adults deliberately contacting children and adolescents online to initiate sexual contact.

"More than 98% of all perpetrators are male, with an average age of about 24; the victims are predominantly female and, on average, just under 12 years old. The primary risk factor is a lack of awareness of the risks associated with one's own online behavior. While teenage perpetrators tend to act out of boredom or just to pass the time, adult perpetrators often have sexual motives and specifically target minors," Münch said, adding that this often leads to a subsequent meeting and abuse.

Young people lack adequate legal protection

Independent Federal Commissioner Against Sexual Abuse of Children and Adolescents Kerstin Claus is calling for better protection, especially for teenagers in Germany

"Instances of 'sextortion' are increasing dramatically, and cybergrooming affects nearly one in three people between the ages of 14 and 17," she said. "And yet it is not currently categorized as a criminal offense."

What is not a criminal offense does not appear in statistics, and what is not counted there is not combated, Claus said. "That is why adolescents must be protected from cybergrooming, and sextortion must be classified as a distinct sexual offense. The federal situation report cannot even begin to reflect the true extent of the sexual violence to which young people are exposed online every day," she warned.

This article has been translated from German.

entries.title: Ousted Turkey opposition leader plans to create a new party
entries.summary: Turkey's main opposition CHP faces a potentially historic rupture after months of infighting over its leadership.
entries.content:
Turkey

Ousted Turkey opposition leader plans to create a new party

July 21, 2026

Turkey's main opposition CHP faces a potentially historic rupture after months of infighting over its leadership.

Turkey's ousted opposition leader, Ozgur Ozel, on Tuesday announced he would resign from the Republican People's Party, or CHP, and launch a new political party.

"Today, we are on the brink of a sad farewell that has become inevitable and whose time has come. However, we would like to share with the people that we carry the hope of a new beginning," Ozel said to more than 80 CHP lawmakers.

Ozel was removed as head of the CHP by court orderin May, in a ruling that also reinstated former leader Kemal Kilicdaroglu as interim leader.

Ozel and Kilicdaroglu disagreed on how to resolve the leadership issue permanently, leading to a dispute within the CHP.

New party did deepens split in Turkish opposition

Ozel's supporters say the case is part of a broader campaign by President Recep Tayyip Erdogan to weaken the opposition, while the government insists Turkey's courts operate independently.

Ozel is reportedly due to submit an application to the Interior Ministry to establish the new party, which, according to reports, will be called the "Yeni Party" ("New Party").

He enjoys strong support from CHP parliamentarians, and many of them are expected to defect from the CHP to join the new party once it is formed.

"Once the necessary procedures are completed, the new party we are about to establish will become the main opposition force in the Grand National Assembly by a wide margin," Ozel said. "We carry within us an unwavering hope for a new beginning," Ozel told lawmakers, who gave him a standing ovation.

Crackdown on the CHP

The dispute comes as investigations targeting CHP-run municipalities continue, including cases against Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu, a prominent rival to President Recep Tayyip Erdogan.

Imamoglu has been imprisoned since March last year and faces multiple criminal cases that could result in decades-long sentences.

After Imamoglu's arrest, Ozel played a key role in mobilizing Turkey's largest street protests in more than a decade, which saw the party's popularity rise in the polls.

Under Ozel's leadership, the CHP also made major gains in the 2024 municipal elections, strengthening its control of key cities including Istanbul and Ankara.

Edited by: Jenipher Camino Gonzalez

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Fact check: Trump wasn't edited out of Spain World Cup photo
entries.summary: After Spain won the FIFA World Cup, the Spanish Football Federation posted a photo of the trophy celebrations, raising questions about whether Donald Trump had been edited out. We took a closer look.
entries.content:
July 21, 2026

After Spain won the FIFA World Cup, the Spanish Football Federation posted a photo of the trophy celebrations, raising questions about whether Donald Trump had been edited out. We took a closer look.

Several posts have been circulating online claiming that US President Donald Trump was edited out of a photo postedby the Spanish Football Federation on its social media accounts.

Claim: One post on the social media platform X gained more than 11 million views while making the same claim.

"Lol Spain edited Trump out of the photo," the caption read.

DW Fact-check: False

The claim is false. The image is neither AI-generated nor altered. We found multiple pieces of evidence online debunking the claim.

There are several videos of Spain's trophy-lifting ceremony available online. In this video, Trump can be seen standing on the podium after handing the FIFA World Cup trophy to the victorious Spanish team, alongside FIFA President Gianni Infantino. Shortly afterwards, Infantino moves to the left while Trump moves towards the right side of the podium.

However, as soon as Infantino realizes that Trump is in the frame, he rushes over to him, seemingly telling him to give the players some space. Several photographers can be seen taking pictures in the foreground.

The photo in question was cropped slightly, which removed Trump and Infantino from the frame, perhaps to focus on the team. Trump was standing some distance away from the players and is therefore not visible in the image. But he was not edited out.

We found the same photo on Picture Alliance (and Newscom,) a photo agency that has distributed images throughout the championship.

Another picture from the same moment was published by the news agency Reuters, but from a different perspective. In this image, taken from a higher angle, Trump and Infantino can clearly be seen standing to the side of the winning team.

(Andreas Wisskirchen contributed to this piece.)

entries.title: DW correspondent Alican Uludag re-arrested in Turkey
entries.summary: The latest arrest comes two months after he was released from detention on charges including "insulting" President Recep Tayyip Erdogan.
entries.content:
July 21, 2026

The latest arrest comes two months after he was released from detention on charges including "insulting" President Recep Tayyip Erdogan.

Deutsche Welle correspondent Alican Uludag has been re-arrested in Turkey on Tuesday, just two months after being granted conditional release.

Uludag announced on social media, "I'm being taken into custody," and Deutsche Welle confirmed his arrest.

"The re-arrest of our colleague Alican Uludag is deeply concerning. Journalistic work must not be criminalized," DW Director General Barbara Massing said, adding, "we expect his rights to be fully respected and demand a fair trial in accordance with the rule of law."

He was taken into custody over an investigation into a post on X criticizing the Turkish judiciary, according to the Ankara Chief Public Prosecutor's Office.

Detained over social media posts

Uludag was first detained in February, accused of insulting President Recep Tayyip Erdogan, spreading misleading information and disparaging state institutions or the Turkish state in 22 social media posts.

"Insulting" or disparaging Erdogan is a criminal offense in Turkey.

He remained in pretrial detention until May 21, when a court granted him conditional release.

Uludag denied all the charges, saying he was objectively reporting.

Uludag draws support

Uludag has, in Turkey's restrictive media landscape, continued to focus on contentious issues such as the judicial system, human rights violations and corruption.

He has "independently researched and reported on the judiciary, politics and society in Turkey in the public interest," Massing said.

During his previous detention, numerous national and international organizations, as well as political representatives, called for his release.

After his initial release, Reporters Without Borders said, "he should not have spent even a single day in detention because of either his reporting or his commentary."

Deutsche Welle said it remained in touch with his lawyers following the latest developments.

Edited by: Jenipher Camino Gonzalez

entries.title: French lawmakers approve social media ban for children
entries.summary: Lawmakers in France gave the green light to a social media ban that could come into effect by the start of the new school year in September, becoming the first EU country to enact such a ban.
entries.content:
July 21, 2026

Lawmakers in France gave the green light to a social media ban that could come into effect by the start of the new school year in September, becoming the first EU country to enact such a ban.

French ​lawmakers Tuesday passed banning access to social media for children under the age of 15. The bill became law after being backed by both French legislative chambers, the Senate and National Assembly.

France's government has touted it as a major legislative step.

"France will become the first country in Europe to introduce a digital age limit to better protect our children online," France's Digital Minister Anne Le Henanff said on X.

The law would add France to a growing list of countries that have already introduced similar restrictions. The restrictions could come into force by the beginning of the new school year in September.

Which measures would the social media ban include?

Under the draft legislation, under 15-year-olds would not be allowed to open a social media account from September 1. Beyond that, platforms would have until January 1, 2027 to close children's existing accounts.

The bill would also ban the use of mobile phones in high schools and require social media platforms to introduce age verification measures in accordance with France's privacy regulator.

One age verification solution could be the age verification app created by the European Commission, and already tested in some EU member states. 

The app works like a third-party service, where users offer an official ID to the app, which, in turn, provides age confirmation to restricted sites or applications.

Social media ban a legislative priority for President Macron

The draft law comes after France's health watchdog warned of the harmful effects for children of using platforms such as TikTok, Instagram and Snapchat in a December report. Officials counted reduced self-esteem and higher exposure to content that is linked to self-harm, suicide and drug consumption as potential risks.

According to the health agency, one in two teenagers spends between two to five hours daily on their smartphone, and 58% use their devices for social media.

The initiative to regulate social media gained momentum as President Macron made it a key part of his domestic agenda in his last year as President.

"We left you in this jungle and it robbed you of your attention," Macron ⁠said to teenagers at a high school in April, while talking about the absence of rules on social networks. "We need to slow down and help you become adults, and above all citizens."

The final vote on Tuesday comes follows deliberations between French legislative chambers, which had previously suggested diverging approaches to the social media ban. While the the Senate preferred a narrower approach which would have created a blacklist banning only certain platforms, the National Assembly favored a broader approach which banned all social networks for children — the latter version was ultimately adopted in the draft legislation.

Could the EU also introduce a social media ban?

Last December, Australia became the first country worldwide to put a social media ban for under 16-year olds in place, and has since been joined by others countries like Indonesia and Turkey.

Though France would be the first EU country to introduce a social media ban plans are underway — or under discussion — in other EU member states, such as Greece, Austria and Slovenia.

Moreover, the bloc as a whole could soon move to introduce social media regulations for minors.

EU Commission president Ursula von der Leyen suggested that the EU's executive body would propose appropriate legal measures after summer.

Speaking during the presentation of an EU-commissioned expert panel on child safety online, von der Leyen for "age-appropriate restrictions" implemented in a "phased and gradual" manner.

Edited by: Jenipher Camino Gonzalez

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: India: Police forcibly remove Rahul Gandhi from protest outside Modi residence
entries.summary: Rahul Gandhi and other leaders of the main opposition Congress party have been dragged away by police from a rally outside PM Narendra Modi's residence, a day after dozens of youth protesters were arrested. Follow DW.
entries.content:
Published July 21, 2026last updated July 21, 2026

Rahul Gandhi and other leaders of the main opposition Congress party have been dragged away by police from a rally outside PM Narendra Modi's residence, a day after dozens of youth protesters were arrested. Follow DW.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • Police forcibly remove Rahul Gandhi and other opposition leaders from a protest outside PM Narendra Modi's residence
  • The protesters are calling for Modi and his home minister, Amit Shah, to step down over a police crackdown against protesters on Monday
  • The mostly young protesters have been calling for the resignation of India's education minister over medical exam paper leaks
  • Modi tells MPs from his ruling coalition that strong action was taken against those involved in the leaks
  • Cockroach Janta Partyfounder Abhijeet Dipke says protesters will not march to the parliament again to protest demonstrators against alleged 'police brutality'
  • The CJP vows to continue the protest until the education minister resigns
  • Activist Sonam Wangchuk, who has been on a hunger strike for 24 days in support of the CJP's demands, is moved to a private hospital

Here are the latest developments from India on Tuesday, July 21, 2026:

Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click here to add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.

Skip next section BJP president accuses Gandhi of 'anarchy,' disrespecting India's institutions

BJP president accuses Gandhi of 'anarchy,' disrespecting India's institutions

The national president of the ruling Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) has accused the leader of the opposition Congress party, Rahul Gandhi, of being a "symbol of anarchy."

In a video statement, Nitin Nabin said the rally that Gandhi had led outside Prime Minister Narendra Modi's residence on Tuesday was "not only unbecoming but also contrary to democratic norms and highly condemnable."

"In a democracy, it is the right of the opposition to protest, but there is a clear difference between protest and anarchy. When protest crosses the boundaries of decency, it becomes anarchy", Nabin said, adding that "Rahul Gandhi has become a symbol of anarchy."

Nabin also accused Gandhi and other Congress leaders of disrespecting the office of the prime minister.

"The way the atmosphere was made tense and disorderly towards the Prime Minister's residence is not just political opposition, but a sign of disrespect for democratic institutions and traditions," he said, calling the incident "unfortunate" and a "black day in India's political history."

Skip next section WATCH: India's cockroach movement undeterred by police violence

WATCH: India's cockroach movement undeterred by police violence

India's cockroach movement is out on the streets for a second day of protest, despite being met by police violence the previous day. As pressure mounts on the government to implement change, protesters vowed not to budge.

Skip next section Congress leaders slam 'anti-democratic' Modi government after detentions

Congress leaders slam 'anti-democratic' Modi government after detentions

Members of the opposition Indian Congress Party have accused the government of Narendra Modi of crushing dissent after Rahul Gandhi and other senior leaders were reportedly detained during a rally outside the prime minister's residence.

Himachal Pradesh Chief Minister Sukhvinder Singh Sukhu denounced the detentions, saying, "It is only when the opposition's voice is heard that it is called a democracy; otherwise, it becomes a dictatorship."

"The strength of democracy lies in dialogue and public trust, not in batons and suppression. It is unfortunate that instead of listening to their voices, the Central Government is adopting a repressive attitude," he said.

Sukhu also called for Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan to resign.

Congress General Secretary KC Venugopal, meanwhile, accused the Modi government of "brutality" and of being "completely anti-democratic."

Congress also posted videos on its official X account that apparently show party leaders Rahul Gandhi and Priyanka Gandhi Vadra after they had been released by police.

It was not immediately clear whether either opposition leader had been charged.

Skip next section WATCH: Is this India's Gen-Z revolution?

WATCH: Is this India's Gen-Z revolution?

Tuesday's scenes outside the prime minister's official residence come a day after violent clashes in New Delhi between police and youth protesters.

DW looks at whether this movement is the start of Gen-Zers taking to the streets to demand change in India, as young people did in many countries across Asia and elsewhere in 2025.

Skip next section IN DEPTH: How India's youth-led 'Cockroach' movement challenges Modi

IN DEPTH: How India's youth-led 'Cockroach' movement challenges Modi

What started as a satirical protest against exam leaks in India has grown into a wider movement voicing young Indians' anger over jobs and education.

Learn more about how the Cockroach movement has grown and how it plans to take on long-time prime minister Narendra Modi in this report.

Skip next section Cockroach party calls off planned protest at police HQ

Cockroach party calls off planned protest at police HQ

The Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) has called off a protest outside police headquarters planned for Tuesday night.

The move comes after police in New Delhi released all the students who were detained during protests on Monday, the party said.

"Huge victory for our movement! The Police has now released all detainees. They had illegally been detained for peacefully protesting for over a day, but the Police has had to bow down to public pressure," the CJP said on X.

Skip next section Rahul Gandhi, opposition leaders detained outside Modi residence

Rahul Gandhi, opposition leaders detained outside Modi residence

Rahul Gandhi and other leaders of the opposition Congress party have been detained outside Prime Minister Narendra Modi's residence, according to DW's Adil Bhat, who is at the scene.

Congress' official X account also shared footage that appeared to show the opposition leader being detained by security officials, accusing Modi of "shameful" treatment of Gandhi and of overseeing a "dictatorship."

As we reported earlier, Gandhi, India's main opposition leader, had called for Modi and Union Home Minister Amit Shah to step down over the police crackdown against youth protesters on Monday.

Thousands of protestors were still camping in and around Jantar Mantar in downtown New Delhi on Tuesday.

The protest outside Modi's residence comes after the Indian parliament was adjourned for a second consecutive day on Tuesday, with the opposition demanding a discussion on exam paper leaks and other issues within India's education system.

Skip next section What else has happened in India today?

What else has happened in India today?

We are taking a brief pause from our coverage of the Cockroach Janta Party protests to give you a roundup of other major developments in India today.

Sikkim tunnel blast leaves 10 dead, several missing

At least 10 people have died in India's Himalayan state of Sikkim after a suspected gas explosion triggered a tunnel collapse.

The incident occurred on Monday at state-owned National Hydroelectric Power Corporation's (NHPC) project on the Teesta river in Samardung village.

Seventeen others are feared trapped, local police chief Sonam Dolma told the media.

The NHPC said that a sudden burst of gas, ‌suspected to be methane trapped or embedded in rocks ​in the tunnel, caused an explosion, dispersing fumes and toxic gas.

Rescuers are struggling to get to those trapped due to the hazardous gases inside the tunnel.

India summons Russian diplomat after Indian seafarers killed in Ukraine ship attack

India on Tuesday summoned a senior Russian diplomat in New Delhi to protest an attack on a cargo ship leaving Ukraine's Black Sea port of Odesa that killed four Indian nationals.

India's foreign ministry said it conveyed "grave concerns and unequivocal condemnation of the attack" to the Russian Chargé d'Affaires Vladimir Ladanov.

Commercial vessel MV Golden Leo was attacked on Sunday. It had five Indian nationals on board.

The ministry said in a statement that it had urged Ladanov to convey to Moscow "India's strong concerns" that "the targeting of commercial shipping and the resulting loss of innocent civilian lives are unacceptable and must be avoided".

Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters that Moscow was in touch with New Delhi on the issue.

"We are explaining our ⁠position. ​Most importantly, our armed forces are striking - ​and will continue to strike - vessels involved in transporting ammunition, weapons, ​and so on for ‌the purposes of the Kyiv regime," Peskov said.

Skip next section Rahul Gandhi demands Modi's resignation for 'destroying the future of India's youth'

Rahul Gandhi demands Modi's resignation for 'destroying the future of India's youth'

India's main opposition leader Rahul Gandhi has called for Prime Minister Narendra Modi and Union Home Minister Amit Shah's resignation over Monday's police crackdown on youth protesters.

Gandhi and other members of his Congress party, including his sister Priyanka, held a demonstration outside Modi's official residence in New Delhi on Tuesday afternoon.

The protest comes after the Indian parliament was adjourned over disruptions for a second day in a row on Tuesday, as the opposition demanded a discussion on paper leaks and other issues within India's education system.

"We have marched to PM Modi's house to demand answers from him for the brutalities against young students yesterday," Gandhi said on X

"The Government doesn't want to take any accountability or does it want to have a debate on it in Parliament. PM and HM must resign for destroying the future of India's youth," he added.

Skip next section Court allows Sonam Wangchuk to move to private hospital — reports

Court allows Sonam Wangchuk to move to private hospital — reports

The Delhi High Court has allowed hunger-striking activist Sonam Wangchuk to be transferred from a government hospital to a private one, following a petition filed by his wife alleging that he was in "illegal detention," legal news websites LiveLaw and Bar and Bench reported on Tuesday.

The court decided that transferring Wangchuk to a hospital of his choice "will be in fulfilment of requirements of ensuring his fundamental rights".

The court directed the private hospital to constitute a team of doctors to "constantly monitor" Wangchuk's medical condition.

In a statement released on Monday night, Wangchuk said, "Seeing [the] police brutality on students, I vow to continue my hunger strike till the youth leaders are allowed to present their grievances in the parliament directly to the MPs."

The 59-year-old has been on a hunger strike since June 28 in support of the Cockroach Janta Party's demands for the Indian education minister's resignation.

On Saturday, police forcibly removed Wangchuk from the stage at Jantar Mantar where he was holding his hunger strike, saying that they were acting on orders of the same court over health concerns.

Strong visuals emerged as police officials, most of whom were not in uniform, climbed onto the stage and led the activist out, shielding him from view using white bedsheets.

Wangchuk's removal from the protest site gave a renewed vigor to the protest movement as thousands of young people arrived at Jantar Mantar over the next two days ahead of Monday's march.

The activist has reportedly continued his hunger protest from the hospital.

Earlier in the day, CJP founder Abhijeet Dipke marked the 24th day of Wangchuk's hunger strike.

Skip next section Health minister meets injured CJP protesters

Health minister meets injured CJP protesters

India’s Union Health Minister J P Nadda on Tuesday met protesters injured in clashes with police during Monday’s Cockroach party protest march as he visited two hospitals in New Delhi, news agency ANI reported.

The minister also met with the doctors to take stock of the conditions of those injured and the medical care they were receiving, ANI reported.

Representatives from the CJP had on Monday handed a written document with their demands to Nadda, marking an opening of talks between the protesters and the Modi government.

Skip next section DW correspondent: Delhi youth protests a 'people's movement'

DW correspondent: Delhi youth protests a 'people's movement'

The protesters who showed up on Delhi's streets on Monday didn't seem like they were necessarily inspired by the Cockroach Janta party or its founder Abhijeet Dipke.

The outpouring on the street on Monday was an opportunity for people across India to express their deep discontent with the government of Prime Minister Narendra Modi — especially the highhandedness with which Sonam Wangchuk [the activist on hunger strike in solidarity with the student protests] was treated. 

Within hours, the protest seemed like it was led by the students themselves; it didn't need instructions from anyone. Despite an internet ban and lack of clarity on the march to parliament, protesters led on, making it truly a people's movement.

The widespread protest is seen as the biggest challenge to Modi's 12-year tenure. Young people have been frustrated over the lack of jobs. The 'Cockroach' movement tapped into that anger that has been simmering for years.

Even as the government has shown an inclination to consider their demands, it may not be enough to immediately calm tempers on the ground. The youth expect a complete overhaul of the education system which has been mired in allegations of corruption.

Skip next section Modi breaks silence on medical exam paper leaks amid 'Cockroach' protest

Modi breaks silence on medical exam paper leaks amid 'Cockroach' protest

Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi has told MPs of his coalition that strong action was taken against those involved in the NEET medical exam paper leaks, Parliamentary Affairs Minister Kiren Rijiju told the media on Tuesday. 

The remarks come a day after youth protesters, angered by a slew of such paper leaks, gathered in massive numbers for a protest walk to the parliament.

Modi brought up the paper leaks at his ruling National Democratic Alliance's (NDA) parliamentary party meeting.

The prime minister said at the meeting that 13 people were arrested and sent to jail after reports of the leaks surfaced, Rijiju said.

"At the same time, to ensure that students' future was not affected, the NEET re-examination was given priority, conducted successfully, and the results were declared without delay," Rijiju quoted Modi as saying.

Modi described the leaks as a "grave sin" and told NDA lawmakers that strict steps have been taken to prevent such paper leaks in the future.

Skip next section What does India's 'Cockroach' movement want?

What does India's 'Cockroach' movement want?

The Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) that started out as a satire movement online two months ago, on Monday was able to galvanize one of the largest anti-government protest rallies India has seen in recent years.

Since June 20, the movement has been holding a sit-in demonstration in New Delhi demanding an overhaul of India's education system.

Protesters are angered by a series of entrance test paper leaks, including those of a crucial medical school entrance exam called National Eligibility cum Entrance Test (NEET).

The NEET paper leak in May forced over two million students to retake the highly-competitive exam, leading to some young aspirants taking their own lives.

Protesters allege that the leaks are a sign of a corruption-ridden education system.

Aside from the education minister's resignation, the CJP's list of demands also include a compensation of 10 million rupees (€91,000 and $104,000) for the families of each of the students who took their own lives.

They also seek the release of prominent social activist Sonam Wangchuk, who was forcibly removed from the protest site in Jantar Mantar and taken to a hospital last Saturday as he observed a days-long hunger-strike in support of the demand.

Skip next section How has the Modi government reacted to the 'cockroach' protest?

How has the Modi government reacted to the 'cockroach' protest?

On Monday, Cockroach Janta Party spokespersons Saurav Das and Ashutosh Ranka met federal Health Minister J P Nadda to formally hand over a written document listing the movement's demands.

Ranka said that the minister had asked for time to discuss the demands with the government.

Nadda on Monday described the talks as having taken place in a "cordial atmosphere" as he urged demonstrators to call off their sit-in protest.

Prime Minister Narendra Modi continues to maintain a silence on the protest.

There has also been no statement on the protest march from Modi's Education Minister, Dharmendra Pradhan, whose resignation the youth protesters are demanding over recurrent examination paper leaks and irregularities in the country's education system.

Pradhan was seen in the Indian Parliament on Monday as the public ire against him poured into the streets leading the house.

entries.title: 'Antifa' song climbs to top of charts after TV show cancellation
entries.summary: German rapper Danger Dan and pianist Igor Levitt had been invited to play a song for the 100th episode of a political satire show on TV. The performance was canceled at the last minute due to its message.
entries.content:
July 21, 2026

German rapper Danger Dan and pianist Igor Levitt had been invited to play a song for the 100th episode of a political satire show on TV. The performance was canceled at the last minute due to its message.

German singer-songwriter Danger Dan and pianist Igor Levit were invited to perform a song titled "Keine Angst” (No Fear) for the 100th episode of a political satire TV show called "Die Anstalt" (The Institution). But shortly before the recording of the program, public broadcaster ZDF withdrew the invitation.

The sudden cancellation is sparking discussions on freedom of artistic expression and the limits of political speech in Germany. It is also drawing attention to the new song, which reached the top of the country's YouTube music charts shortly after the musicians decried the cancellation on June 17.

The broadcaster argued that parts of the lyrics could be interpreted as endorsing political extremism, vigilantism or violence against far-right activists, noting that this would conflict with ZDF's programming guidelines.

Show hosts condemn cancellation

"Die Anstalt" combines in-depth, single-topic investigations and political comedy. In the special 100th episode, titled "Die ANTIFAnstalt," the comedians analyzed the program of the far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD), which is currently the most popular political party in Germany, all while civil rights groups are assessing it for potential unconstitutionality and ban proceedings.

The episode airs on television on Tuesday evening and is already available online.

The cancellation was not left uncommented during the show.

Instead of the song, an empty stage with a piano and microphones was shown during the program. After a few seconds of dead air, the hosts discussed the controversy, noting that the lyrics had been submitted to the broadcasting directors weeks ahead of the recording and that their plan was to openly discuss the lyrics with the author after the performance.

"We understand that opinions on the song may be divided, but we simply consider ZDF's decision to be spineless — especially at a time when right-wing extremist violence has once again reached a record high in the history of the Federal Republic of Germany," said one of the show hosts, Claus von Wagner.

The team took turns reading out and commenting on passages from the song, pointing out the sections that could be interpreted as criminal offenses or calls to violence.

What the song is about

The song offers a step-by-step guide on how to organize local, leaderless structures aimed at countering far-right movements. It advocates strict operational security, such as avoiding digital footprints and relying on face-to-face communication. It also describes tactics for investigating political opponents and publicizing their identities and affiliations, a practice often referred to as "outing" or doxing.

The lyrics also include references to militant action and express solidarity with individuals who have been found guilty of leading violent attacks on neo-Nazis.

ZDF director Norbert Himmler said he stood by the decision to cancel the performance: "In my view, the lyrics unquestionably provide instructions for a leftist resistance willing to use violence," he told the German daily Süddeutsche Zeitung.

At the same time, he admitted that his station should have handled the case differently: "We can be faulted for the last-minute cancellation," he said, adding, "We should have asked ourselves basic questions earlier — ideally before inviting the artists."

Eight members of the ZDF Television Council have criticized the broadcaster over the cancellation. In an open letter published on Monday by the online outlet netzpolitik.org, the council members argued that ZDF missed an opportunity to spark a debate about right-wing extremism in Germany and the various ways to combat it.

Danger Dan: Testing the limits of freedom of art

Danger Dan, whose real name is Daniel Pongratz, is known for his politically charged lyrics.

He is active as a member of the hip-hop band Antilopen Gang and as a solo artist. Lyrics on his debut solo album from 2008 already dealt with antisemitism in German rap.

His best-known hit came out in 2021. The single "Das ist alles von der Kunstfreiheit gedeckt" (That's All Covered by Freedom of Artistic Expression) deliberately tests the boundaries between protected artistic expression and potentially defamatory speech, and sharply criticizes right-wing politicians and public figures.

Igor Levit is a globally renowned pianist who is also widely recognized for his social activism, political engagement and public commentary. He had already accompanied Danger Dan on the piano in April 2021 for the live TV performance of the protest song "Das ist alles von der Kunstfreiheit gedeckt" on another program, "ZDF Magazin Royale."

Danger Dan and Igor Levit are now offering a substitute concert on Tuesday evening at Berlin's Columbia Theater. The show, "Die keine Angststalt" (a wordplay combining the title of the song with that of the TV show), will also be broadcast on YouTube.

According to reports, proceeds from ticket sales will go to the association Opferperspektive, described as "the central point of contact in Brandenburg [the state surrounding Berlin] for victims of right-wing, racist and antisemitic violence."

Edited by: Josh Lang

entries.title: Germany: Mass shooting suspect found dead in prison
entries.summary: A man suspected of carrying out one of the worst mass shootings in recent German history was found dead in his cell after an apparent suicide.
entries.content:
July 21, 2026

A man suspected of carrying out one of the worst mass shootings in recent German history was found dead in his cell after an apparent suicide.

The Justice Ministry of Germany's state of Lower Saxony confirmed on Tuesday that the man suspected of killing six people at a youth and womens' shelter in the town of Stade last month had died by suicide.

Identified as Fatih Khan G. due to Germany's privacy laws, the ministry said there was "no indication of third-party involvement" in his death.

What happened in Stade?

G. is believed to have opened fire at the center for mothers and children, killing six welfare workers.

According to authorities, his three-month older daughter and her 34-year-old month were residing there. He had been in a custody dispute with his former partner over the baby, who showed signs of shaken baby syndrome.

He had reportedly previously threatened a doctor at a medical practice where his daughter had been seen.

He came to the shelter on June 29 to allegedly discuss accusations he had physically abused the baby. G. attempted to flee in a car but was caught shortly thereafter by police.

Edited by: Jenipher Camino Gonzalez

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: China narrows AI gap, challenges US influence
entries.summary: At the World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, Beijing showcased advances in AI while promoting its vision of open-source development and global governance.
entries.content:
China

China narrows AI gap, challenges US influence

July 21, 2026

At the World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, Beijing showcased advances in AI while promoting its vision of open-source development and global governance.

As China narrows the artificial intelligence (AI) gap with the United States, Beijing is promoting a narrative centered on "open source" and "inclusive AI" as an argument for its vision of global governance.

During his keynote speech at the World Artificial Intelligence Conference (WAIC) in Shanghai on Friday, Chinese President Xi Jinping expressed China's ambition to take a leading role in setting global standards in AI. He criticized what he described as the "overextension of the concept of national security."

Xi did not openly reference the United States. However, Beijing's effort to position itself as an advocate of a new AI order and challenge US dominance was unmistakable.

This year's Shanghai summit sent several important signals, says Rogier Creemers, assistant professor at Leiden University in the Netherlands specializing in China's digital technology policy. The WAIC indicates that artificial intelligence is now one of Beijing's political priorities, and that China intends to compete with the United States by supporting open-source AI models and promoting AI-driven industrialization, Creemers told DW. He also noted China hopes to win support from countries in the Global South for this effort.

Beijing closing the AI gap

China is steadily narrowing the gap with the United States in AI model development, and its efforts at AI application in industrial processes are slowly maturing.

Chinese AI model developers have continued to keep pace on the global stage, largely by training open-weight models on limited but advanced computer hardware, Paul Triolo, senior vice president for China and tech policy lead at DGA-Albright Stonebridge Group, told DW.

Triolo believes deployment of AI across Chinese enterprises may be progressing faster than in the United States.

In addition, there is less fear of AI among the Chinese population than there is in the US, facilitating more rapid uptake of AI applications at the consumer level.

Earlier this year, Chinese state media claimed that the performance gap between Chinese and US AI models had "largely disappeared."

Several experts broadly agreed with this assessment, saying that certain benchmark tests of China's large language models (LLMs) such as GLM-5.2 and Kimi K3 showed the gap narrowing or even fully closing.

For most applications, Triolo noted, China's leading open-weight AI models are cheaper to run and more than capable of handling most tasks.

At the same time, advanced proprietary models from leading US companies such as Anthropic, OpenAI and Google still retain advantages in areas such as security and scalability.

Has the US 'AI firewall' worked?

In January 2025, the US Commerce Department expanded AI-related export controls through its "Framework for Artificial Intelligence Diffusion," covering advanced computing chips, certain AI model weights and access to large-scale AI computing infrastructure.

The US also boosted export controls on advanced chips and semiconductor manufacturing equipment intended for China.

Laila Khawaja, research director at Gavekal Research, told DW that the US has been partially successful.

The controls have indeed impacted China's ability to manufacture chips domestically, cheaply, and at scale.

As for export controls on advanced AI chips, however, Khawaja notes that a significant amount of chips is still entering China through the gray market, while Chinese firms can still access computing resources through overseas cloud services.

According to Triolo, the evidence so far suggests the US strategy of slowing down China's advanced AI development has failed at many levels. At the same time, it caused massive collateral damage to US companies, especially to to their budgets for research and development and their supply chains, such as those delivering rare earths.

China's open-source AI push

In his keynote speech, Xi emphasized the importance of "encouraging open-source development."

Chinese state media have likewise promoted the message that China "does not pursue technological blockades."

Analysts say Beijing is increasingly framing "open source" and "inclusive AI" as its own narrative to counter the United States' largely closed-source AI ecosystem.

Chinese AI models are largely open-weight, with leading offerings such as DeepSeek, Zhipu's GLM 5.2 and Kimi K3 released under MIT licenses that allow users to download, deploy, modify, distribute and commercialize them.

Creemers noted that, for the time being, Chinese AI models are quite open in weight, but slightly less open in source.

According to Creemers, China hopes to encourage the rest of the world to adopt Chinese AI models, thereby creating overseas markets for Chinese technology and ensuring that China remains part of the next wave of technological development.

China's WAICO challenges Pax Silica

In Shanghai, Xi also announced that the World AI Cooperation Organization (WAICO), an initiative launched by China, has already attracted 29 member countries.

He described the organization as "an important milestone in the history of AI development" and said it responds to calls from Global South countries for a greater role in AI governance.

WAICO is seen as an initiative to counter the US-led Pax Silica international initiative which aims to secure ​global AI and critical mineral supply chains.

The real competition, according to Triolo, lies in which parts of the US and Chinese AI technology stacks are ultimately adopted by countries in the Global South.

He believes China holds a significant advantage due to lower costs and the extensive digital infrastructure already built by Chinese companies such as Huawei across many developing countries.

Looking ahead

Overall, experts believe that the US will continue to lead in AI hardware design and compute capacity, while China could seize the lead on model innovation and will come to lead on AI diffusion.

Creemers argued that China's biggest structural disadvantage remains the lack of trust from countries in the Global North.

At the same time, US export controls continue to leave Chinese companies facing shortages of advanced AI computing hardware, limiting their ability to rapidly scale inference capacity to meet growing demand.

Looking ahead, Creemers argues that the rise of China's AI sector represents a broader historical shift.

"We need to understand that we are now facing, for the first time in modern history, a non-Western technological superpower," he said.

In his view, the future will not be defined by a singular global technological sphere, but rather by "a complex of overlapping technological networks and processes."

Edited by: Keith Walker
